items.q: What does voltage (potential difference) across a component represent?
items.correct: The energy transferred per unit charge passing through that component.
items.misconception: Voltage is the 'amount of electricity' flowing through the wire.
items.random_wrong: Voltage measures how many electrons are stored in a battery.
items.why: Students conflate voltage with current because both are 'something to do with electricity'; they don't distinguish 'energy per charge' from 'rate of charge flow'.

items.q: What happens to the total resistance when you add another resistor in parallel?
items.correct: Total resistance decreases — there are more paths for current to flow.
items.misconception: Adding any resistor always increases the total resistance.
items.random_wrong: Adding a parallel resistor doubles the voltage available.
items.why: Students apply the series rule (resistances add) to parallel circuits because they don't visualise multiple paths reducing the overall opposition.

items.q: Does Hooke's law apply to a spring for any amount of stretching?
items.correct: No — it only holds up to the limit of proportionality; beyond that the relationship between force and extension is no longer linear.
items.misconception: Yes — Hooke's law applies to any spring at any extension.
items.random_wrong: Only when the force is small enough to be ignored.
items.why: Students apply F = kx universally without noting the limit of proportionality, leading to over-prediction at large extensions.

items.q: A 12 V supply drives 4 A through a resistor. What is the resistance?
items.why: It's 3 Ω. “48 Ω” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: In a series circuit with two identical bulbs:
items.misconception: each bulb gets full supply voltage
items.why: The answer examiners want is: voltage is shared equally. “each bulb gets full supply voltage” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: What charge flows when 3 A passes for 5 seconds?
items.why: The real figure is 15 C. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
